items.foco: Risk factors: chronic anovulation and obesity
items.title: Risk factors
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors

  • Chronic anovulation (polycystic ovarian syndrome, perimenopause) generates estrogen exposure without progestational opposition, stimulating endometrial proliferation.
  • Obesity (body mass index >30 kg/m²) increases the peripheral conversion of androgens to estrone by the aromatase enzyme in the tissue adipose.
  • Other factors: nulliparity, late menopause (>55 years), type 2 diabetes mellitus, arterial hypertension and use of tamoxifen.
  • The relative risk of endometrial hyperplasia in obesity is 3-4 times greater compared to normal weight.

items.foco: Classification: hyperplasia without atypia vs with atypia
items.title: Histological classification
items.contentHtml:
Histological classification

  • The current classification (World Health Organization 2014) divides endometrial hyperplasia into two categories: hyperplasia without atypia and hyperplasia with atypia.
  • Hyperplasia without atypia shows glandular proliferation with irregular architecture but without cytological atypia; risk of progression to endometrial cancer <5% in 20 years.
  • Hyperplasia with atypia presents nuclear atypia (enlarged nuclei, thick chromatin, prominent nucleoli); risk of concomitant cancer or progression up to 30-50%.
  • The presence of atypia is the most important predictor of malignancy and defines the therapeutic approach.

items.title: Definitive diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Definitive diagnosis: endometrial biopsy

  • The diagnosis of endometrial hyperplasia requires endometrial biopsy with histological evaluation; cytology (Pap smear) is not sufficient.
  • Obtaining methods: aspiration biopsy (Pipelle cannula) in the office, fractionated uterine curettage or hysteroscopy with directed biopsy.
  • Pipelle biopsy has sensitivity >90% for endometrial cancer, but may underestimate focal atypical hyperplasia; hysteroscopy is the gold standard for focal lesions.
  • In postmenopausal women with bleeding, biopsy is indicated if endometrial thickness is >4-5 mm on transvaginal ultrasound.

items.foco: Abnormal uterine bleeding is the main symptom
items.title: Main symptom
items.contentHtml:
Abnormal uterine bleeding

  • The cardinal symptom of endometrial hyperplasia is abnormal uterine bleeding: intermenstrual bleeding, menorrhagia, or postmenopausal bleeding.
  • In postmenopausal women, any bleeding (even spotting) requires ruling out endometrial hyperplasia or cancer.
  • In premenopausal women, The suspicion arises in the event of persistent irregular cycles, heavy bleeding or intermenstrual bleeding without obvious organic cause.
  • Bleeding is caused by vascular fragility of the hyperplastic endometrium and irregular peeling.

items.title: Transvaginal ultrasound
items.contentHtml:
Transvaginal ultrasound: endometrial thickness

  • Transvaginal ultrasound is the first imaging test; measures endometrial thickness (double layer) in the sagittal plane.
  • In postmenopausal women, an endometrial thickness <4 mm has negative predictive value >99% for cancer; >5 mm requires biopsy.
  • In premenopause, the thickness varies with the cycle; There is no absolute threshold, but >16 mm in the follicular phase or >12 mm postmenstrual is considered abnormal.
  • Additional findings: heterogeneous echogenicity, intramural cysts, or irregular thickening suggest hyperplasia or neoplasia.

items.foco: Treatment: progestogens for hyperplasia without atypia
items.title: Medical treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment: progestins in hyperplasia without atypia

  • In hyperplasia without atypia, the first-line treatment is progestins: medroxyprogesterone acetate (10-20 mg/day orally, 12-14 days per cycle) or levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device (52 mg, every 5 years).
  • The device Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine has a higher remission rate (>90%) than the oral route and is preferred in women without desire for fertility.
  • The duration of treatment is 3-6 months, followed by control endometrial biopsy to confirm remission.
  • In women with desire for fertility, cyclical or continuous progestogens can be used, with strict monitoring.

items.foco: Hyperplasia with atypia: risk of endometrial cancer
items.title: Endometrial cancer risk
items.contentHtml:
Hyperplasia with atypia: risk of cancer

  • Hyperplasia with atypia is considered a direct precursor lesion of endometrial cancer type I (endometrioid).
  • The risk of concomitant endometrial cancer (in the hysterectomy specimen) is 30-50%, and progression without treatment occurs in approximately 25% at 5 years.
  • The presence of cytological atypia is the strongest risk factor; the complex architecture increases the additional risk.
  • Therefore, the standard management is surgical (hysterectomy), except in women who wish to preserve fertility, where high-dose progestogens can be considered with close monitoring.

items.foco: Hysterectomy is definitive treatment for atypia
items.title: Hysterectomy
items.contentHtml:
Hysterectomy: definitive treatment

  • In hyperplasia with atypia, total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is the definitive treatment, since it eliminates the tissue at risk and allows diagnosis of occult cancer.
  • Hysterectomy is indicated in women without desire for fertility, postmenopausal or with acceptable surgical risk factors.
  • Intraoperative study (freezing) is recommended if there is suspicion of cancer. invader; Surgery may include lymphadenectomy if carcinoma is confirmed.
  • In cases of high surgical risk, high-dose progestogens (megestrol acetate 160 mg/day) with control biopsy every 3-6 months can be chosen.

items.foco: Tamoxifen increases risk of endometrial hyperplasia
items.title: Tamoxifen
items.contentHtml:
Tamoxifen and endometrial risk

  • The use of tamoxifen (selective estrogen receptor modulator) in breast cancer increases the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and endometrial cancer (relative risk 2-3 times).
  • Tamoxifen acts as an estrogen agonist in the endometrium, stimulating glandular proliferation.
  • In asymptomatic women who receive tamoxifen, routine ultrasound screening is not recommended; It is only indicated for abnormal uterine bleeding.
  • Endometrial thickness on ultrasound is usually >5 mm; biopsy or hysteroscopy are required if there is significant bleeding or thickening (>8 mm).

items.foco: Lynch syndrome is associated genetic factor
items.title: Lynch syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Lynch Syndrome

  • Lynch syndrome (hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer) is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by mutations in mismatch repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2).
  • It confers a high risk of endometrial cancer (up to 40-60% during life), often preceded by hyperplasia. endometrial.
  • In women with Lynch syndrome, endometrial screening with annual biopsy or prophylactic hysterectomy after completing fertility is recommended.
  • Endometrial hyperplasia in this context may be more aggressive and of earlier onset (average 40-50 years).

items.foco: Suspicion in postmenopausal women with bleeding
items.title: Clinical suspicion
items.contentHtml:
Suspicion in postmenopausal women

  • In postmenopausal women, any abnormal uterine bleeding (postmenopausal) is an indication for immediate evaluation to rule out endometrial hyperplasia or cancer.
  • The prevalence of endometrial hyperplasia in this group is 5-15%, and of endometrial cancer of 5-10%.
  • Transvaginal ultrasound is the first step: endometrial thickness <4 mm rules out with high confidence; >5 mm requires endometrial biopsy.
  • Additional risk factors: advanced age (>65 years), obesity, diabetes, use of tamoxifen and history of Lynch syndrome.

items.foco: Follow-up: repeated biopsy after medical treatment
items.title: Follow-up
items.contentHtml:
Follow-up after medical treatment

  • After 3-6 months of treatment with progestogens in hyperplasia without atypia, a control endometrial biopsy should be performed to confirm remission (atrophic or secretory endometrium).
  • If hyperplasia persists without atypia, another cycle of progestins can be repeated or a change to a progestin-releasing intrauterine device can be performed. levonorgestrel.
  • In hyperplasia with medically treated atypia (due to fertility desire), the biopsy should be repeated every 3-6 months; If there is no remission in 6-12 months, hysterectomy is recommended.
  • After remission, follow-up should continue with an annual biopsy or in the event of new bleeding, given the risk of recurrence (up to 30% at 5 years).
